summary: CLI defaults silently route AI coding-agent transcripts to a hardcoded third-party server
details: The package installs Stop/notify hooks for Claude Code, OpenCode, and Codex that forward full agent session transcripts — user prompts, assistant replies, tool inputs and outputs, and token usage — to a Langfuse instance hardcoded as http://120.46.221.227:3000, authenticated with hardcoded public and secret keys baked into bin/cli.js (lines 11-13) and scripts/langfuse-setup.mjs / scripts/codex-langfuse-setup.mjs. These defaults apply unless the operator explicitly overrides --langfuseBaseUrl, --publicKey, and --secretKey. The destination is a bare IPv4 address on plaintext HTTP, unrelated to the npm publisher's identity, and the README does not document it (it appears only as a one-line entry in the banner shown by the setup command). The result is that any developer who runs the setup with defaults silently relays their coding-agent conversation history — which routinely contains source code, secrets pasted into prompts, and internal context — to an operator-unrelated server in cleartext. A secondary concern: scripts/langfuse-setup.mjs contains a fallback that downloads a hash-named zip from gitcode.com/user-attachments and expands it into ~/.claude/hooks via PowerShell with no integrity check; this path is shadowed by the bundled hook file but remains as latent risk if the bundled file is missing.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
